grammar_topics.explanation_en: Countable nouns refer to things that can be counted. They have both singular and plural forms.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Danh từ đếm được chỉ những thứ có thể đếm được. Chúng có cả hình thái số ít và số nhiều.
rules: Use singular or plural verbs depending on the noun form.
Some have irregular plurals (e.g., person/people, mouse/mice).
Some do not change in plural form (e.g., sheep, deer).
Hyphenated nouns pluralize the key word (e.g., brothers-in-law).
Collective nouns (group, team, government) can take either a singular or plural verb.
Specific phrases describe groups: a flock of birds/sheep, a herd of cows, a pack of cards/dogs, a bunch of flowers, a set of keys.

examples.translation: Bức tranh đó thật tuyệt vời.

examples.sentence: The government are not doing anything to help the arts.
examples.translation: Chính phủ không làm gì để hỗ trợ nghệ thuật.

grammar_topics.explanation_en: These nouns only have a singular form and always take singular verbs. They cannot be counted directly.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Những danh từ này chỉ có hình thái số ít và luôn đi với động từ số ít. Chúng không thể được đếm trực tiếp.
rules: Common examples: advice, furniture, hair, information, jewellery, knowledge, luggage, news, permission, water.
Use phrases like 'a piece of', 'a bar of', 'a loaf of' to refer to individual items.
Common phrases: a bar of chocolate, a bit of help, a blade of grass, a breath of fresh air, a drop of water, a grain of salt, a gust of wind, a loaf of bread, a sheet of paper, a speck of dust.

examples.translation: Thông tin đó có đáng tin cậy không?

examples.sentence: Could I have a slice of bread?
examples.translation: Cho tôi một lát bánh mì được không?

grammar_topics.explanation_en: These nouns only have a plural form and always take plural verbs. Many represent items made of two parts.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Những danh từ này chỉ có hình thái số nhiều và luôn đi với động từ số nhiều. Nhiều danh từ trong số này chỉ những vật gồm hai phần.
rules: Common examples: arms, binoculars, cattle, clothes, earnings, goods, groceries, jeans, pyjamas, savings, scales, scissors, shorts, trousers.
Use 'a pair of' for items with two parts (e.g., a pair of scissors/trousers).
Watch out: Some nouns ending in -s are singular (e.g., news, physics, politics, diabetes).

examples.sentence: The scissors aren't on the table.
examples.translation: Cái kéo không có ở trên bàn.
Vật lý là môn học yêu thích của tôi.

grammar_topics.explanation_en: Many nouns can be countable with one meaning and uncountable with another.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Nhiều danh từ có thể là danh từ đếm được với nghĩa này và là danh từ không đếm được với nghĩa khác.
rules: Wood (uncountable = material; countable = a small forest).
Paper (uncountable = material; countable = a newspaper/document).
Hair (uncountable = all the hair on a head; countable = individual strands).
Work (uncountable = general activity/job; countable = a creation like 'a work of art').

examples.sentence: The table is made of wood.
examples.translation: Cái bàn được làm bằng gỗ.

examples.sentence: It's a picture of a local wood.
examples.translation: Đó là bức ảnh về một khu rừng nhỏ ở địa phương.

grammar_topics.explanation_en: Words used to express quantity, categorized by the type of noun they modify.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Các từ dùng để chỉ số lượng, được phân loại theo loại danh từ mà chúng bổ nghĩa.
rules: Countable only: a couple of, a number of, another, both, each, either, every, (a) few, many, several.
Singular uncountable only: an amount of, a great deal of, (a) little, much.
Both: all, any, enough, a lot of/lots of, more, most, no, none, plenty of, some.
Distinction: 'a few/a little' means some (positive); 'few/little' means not many/much (negative).

examples.translation: Tôi có một vài người bạn (đủ dùng).
Tôi có ít bạn (không đủ, cảm thấy cô đơn).

grammar_topics.topic: Articles: A, An, The & Zero Article
grammar_topics.explanation_en: Rules for using indefinite, definite, or no articles in different contexts.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Quy tắc sử dụng mạo từ bất định, xác định hoặc không dùng mạo từ trong các ngữ cảnh khác nhau.
rules: A/An: Used for non-specific singular countable nouns or mentioning something for the first time.
The: Used for specific things, unique items (the moon), superlatives (the best), or groups of people (the young).
Zero Article: Used for general plural/uncountable nouns, concepts (society, nature), or specific categories like transport (by car), meals (at dinner), and institutions (go to school as a student).

examples.sentence: The guitar is a musical instrument.
examples.translation: Đàn ghi-ta là một nhạc cụ.

examples.sentence: I go to work by train.
examples.translation: Tôi đi làm bằng tàu hỏa.

exercises.label: A
exercises.instruction: Circle the correct word. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Are those ___ or goats in that field over there?
items.explanation: 'Sheep' is the same in both singular and plural forms.

items.sentence: How many ___ have you got?
items.explanation: In hyphenated nouns, we pluralize the key word (sister).

items.sentence: Look at those lovely little ___!
items.explanation: Both are correct; 'fish' is the usual plural, 'fishes' refers to different species.

items.sentence: I've only got one important piece of ___ to tell you, I think.
items.explanation: 'News' is an uncountable noun that ends in -s but takes a singular verb.

items.sentence: The hairdresser quickly swept up the ___ on the floor.
items.explanation: 'Hair' is uncountable when referring to a mass of hair.

items.sentence: I'll be giving you a lot of ___ so do please take notes.
items.explanation: 'Information' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: We've bought quite a lot of ___ in the last few days.
items.explanation: 'Furniture' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: Could you give us both ___ to miss PE tomorrow?
items.explanation: 'Permission' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: ___ is my favourite subject by far.
items.explanation: The subject is 'Physics' (ends in -s but is singular).

items.sentence: I think I've got some ___ on me.
items.explanation: 'Money' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: I'm going to give them all the ___ I can.
items.explanation: 'Advice' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: What kind of ___ are you interested in getting?
items.explanation: 'Jewellery' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: Would you like some ___?
items.explanation: Both are correct: 'chocolate' as a substance (uncountable) or 'chocolates' as individual pieces (countable).

items.sentence: You've got to have very good general ___ to go on Who Wants To Be A Millionaire.
items.explanation: 'Knowledge' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: I've always been interested in ___.
items.explanation: 'Politics' ends in -s but is singular.

exercises.label: B
exercises.instruction: Complete using the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: ___ (be) all the furniture very expensive when you got it?
items.explanation: 'Furniture' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: My earnings ___ (increase) considerably over the last two years.
items.explanation: 'Earnings' is a plural uncountable noun and the time 'over the last two years' requires the present perfect.

items.sentence: ___ (be) all the luggage yours?
items.explanation: 'Luggage' is a singular uncountable noun.

items.sentence: Your pyjamas ___ (be) on your bed, I think.
items.explanation: 'Pyjamas' is a plural uncountable noun.

items.sentence: Politics ___ (be) a love of mine, as well as my profession.
items.explanation: 'Politics' is treated as a singular noun.

items.sentence: ___ (be) your team doing well in the league right now?
items.explanation: 'Team' is a collective noun and can take a singular or plural verb.

items.sentence: The people over there ___ (need) serving.
items.explanation: 'People' is the plural of 'person'.

items.sentence: ___ (be) the news good or bad?
items.explanation: 'News' is singular.

items.sentence: There ___ (be) blood all over the floor!
items.explanation: 'Blood' is singular uncountable.

items.sentence: The cattle ___ (eat) at the moment.
items.explanation: 'Cattle' is a plural uncountable noun.

items.sentence: Diabetes ___ (be) very common these days.
items.explanation: 'Diabetes' is a disease name ending in -s but singular.

items.sentence: Where ___ (be) the scissors?
items.explanation: 'Scissors' is a plural uncountable noun.

items.sentence: My work ___ (be) the most important thing in my life.
items.explanation: 'Work' (general activity) is singular uncountable.

items.sentence: I think the milk ___ (go off). Shall I pour it away?
items.explanation: 'Milk' is singular uncountable.

items.sentence: The jeans I tried on in the shop yesterday ___ (be) far too small.
items.explanation: 'Jeans' is a plural uncountable noun.

exercises.label: C
exercises.instruction: If a word in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is in the wrong sentence, write the correct word on the line.

items.sentence: I'm off to the newsagent's to get a *pair* of chocolate.
items.explanation: We say 'a bar of chocolate'.

items.sentence: Could you get me a *flock* of chewing gum when you go to the shops?
items.explanation: Chewing gum usually comes in a 'pack' or 'stick'.

items.sentence: I think I'll have another *herd* of toast.
items.explanation: We say 'a slice/piece of toast'.

items.sentence: Just add a *breath* of milk if the mixture starts to get a bit dry.
items.explanation: We say 'a drop of milk'.

items.sentence: I'm popping out for a *pack* of fresh air.
items.explanation: We say 'a breath of fresh air'.

items.sentence: There was a *block* of cows being taken down the lane for milking.
items.explanation: A group of cows is a 'herd'.

items.sentence: You said you wanted to get a new *bar* of trousers, didn't you?
items.explanation: We say 'a pair of trousers'.

items.sentence: Get us a *bunch* of bread when you go to the shops, won't you?
items.explanation: A whole unit of bread is a 'loaf'.

items.sentence: I was thinking of getting Jill a *blade* of flowers for our anniversary.
items.explanation: A group of flowers is a 'bunch'.

items.sentence: There was a really strong *drop* of wind and it blew a man's wig off!
items.explanation: A sudden strong wind is a 'gust'.

items.sentence: My mum's so houseproud; there's never a *gust* of dust anywhere in the house.
items.explanation: A tiny piece of dust is a 'speck'.

items.sentence: Could you cut me off a small *slice* of that cheese?
items.explanation: Correct usage.

items.sentence: That *speck* of birds will be migrating south for the winter.
items.explanation: A group of birds is a 'flock'.

items.sentence: Since we got the goat, there's not a *loaf* of grass longer than about two centimetres!
items.explanation: A single piece of grass is a 'blade'.

items.sentence: We do like a nice *bit* of steak every now and again, don't we, Francis?
items.explanation: Correct usage.

items.sentence: How heavy's that *sheet* of concrete, would you say?
items.explanation: Large solid concrete is a 'block'.

items.sentence: We'll give you a *set* of keys and a security pass.
items.explanation: Correct usage.

items.sentence: There's not a *grain* of truth in what she said.
items.explanation: Correct usage.

items.sentence: Is one *piece* of sugar enough?
items.explanation: Sugar in a cube/solid form is a 'lump'.

items.sentence: I'll tear you off a *lump* of paper if you're going to take notes.
items.explanation: Paper comes in a 'sheet' or 'scrap'.

exercises.label: D
exercises.instruction: Complete the pairs of sentences using the correct form of a word in the box.

items.sentence: Right! I'm off to ___. / How many ___ of art by Picasso can you actually name?
items.explanation: 'Work' is uncountable for a job; 'works' refers to specific artistic creations.

items.sentence: The storm did quite a lot of ___, so I hear. / The jury awarded them ___ of over €500,000.
items.explanation: 'Damage' is uncountable for harm; 'damages' refers to legal compensation.

items.sentence: I don't think I've ever baked three ___ from scratch in one day. / Would you like another piece of ___?
items.explanation: 'Cakes' are whole items; 'cake' is the substance.

items.sentence: Thanks, you've been a great ___. / Without your ___, we don't know what we'd have done.
items.explanation: 'A help' can be used for a helpful person/event; 'help' is the general uncountable concept.

items.sentence: Ed was looking in the mirror and noticed one of his ___ had gone grey. / I gather that long ___ is back in fashion amongst the young.
items.explanation: 'Hairs' are individual strands; 'hair' is the whole mass.

items.sentence: Did you know that ___ is actually a kind of liquid? / I can't find my ___. Have you seen them anywhere?
items.explanation: 'Glass' is the material; 'glasses' are spectacles.

items.sentence: Is your left ___ really longer than your right? / The ___ trade is an industry I'd rather have nothing to do with.
items.explanation: 'Arm' is the body part; 'arms' refers to weapons.

items.sentence: Let's have a picnic in the ___. / That table's not made of ___. It's plastic!
items.explanation: 'Wood' (countable/plural) is a small forest; 'wood' (uncountable) is material.

items.sentence: Phil's ___ of eighteenth century pottery is astounding. / Phil's got a great ___ of eighteenth century pottery.
items.explanation: General knowledge is uncountable; 'a knowledge of' is an idiomatic countable usage.

items.sentence: No one can hear you scream in ___ because there's no sound. / Why are there no parking ___ round here at all?
items.explanation: 'Space' is outer space; 'spaces' are physical areas for cars.

items.sentence: How did people write things down before ___ was invented? / The story appeared in most of the Sunday ___.
items.explanation: 'Paper' is material; 'papers' are newspapers.

items.sentence: One of our ___ is ill so we've had to call the vet out. / This ___ tastes delicious!
items.explanation: 'Chickens' are the animals; 'chicken' is the meat.

items.sentence: How many ___ have I told you not to do that? / What ___ did you get home last night?
items.explanation: 'Times' refers to occurrences; 'time' refers to the hour.

items.sentence: Let's get a big bar of milk ___. / Oh no! There are only two ___ left in the box!
items.explanation: 'Chocolate' is the substance; 'chocolates' are individual pieces.

exercises.label: E
exercises.instruction: Complete each second sentence using one of the two words in bold and any other words you need so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence: Only two boys volunteered to hand out programmes. -> Only ___ boys volunteered to hand out programmes.
items.explanation: 'A couple of' means two.

items.sentence: There were quite a few people at the party who I knew. -> There were ___ people at the party who I knew.
items.explanation: 'A number of' is used with countable nouns like 'people'.

items.sentence: When I was small, I'd never heard of computers. -> When I was ___ girl, I'd never heard of computers.
items.explanation: 'A little' here means 'small' when used with 'girl'.

items.sentence: There's not much ketchup left in the bottle. -> There's only ___ ketchup left in the bottle.
items.explanation: 'A little' is used with uncountable nouns like 'ketchup' to mean 'some'.

items.sentence: Some kids have put their names down already. -> ___ kids have put their names down already.
items.explanation: 'A few' is used with countable nouns to mean 'some'.

items.sentence: I haven't got much money on me, I'm afraid. -> I've only got ___ money on me, I'm afraid.
items.answer: a small amount of / a certain amount of
items.explanation: 'Amount' is used with uncountable nouns like 'money'.

items.sentence: I didn't understand a lot of what she said, to be honest. -> I didn't understand ___ what she said, to be honest.
items.explanation: 'Much' is used for uncountable quantities (what she said).

items.sentence: Not enough British people can speak a foreign language. -> ___ British people can speak a foreign language.
items.explanation: 'Few' (without 'a') means 'not many', implying it is not enough.

exercises.label: F
exercises.instruction: Circle the correct word or phrase. More than one option may be correct in each sentence.

items.sentence: Would you like ___ coffee?
items.explanation: Both are possible in questions; 'some' suggests an offer.

items.sentence: I've hardly got ___ money left!
items.explanation: 'Hardly any' is a common expression meaning almost none.

items.sentence: ___ of the ducks have names.
items.explanation: Both 'A few' and 'Only a few' work grammatically with 'of the'.

items.sentence: I've only got ___ time, so make it quick!
items.answer: a little / a small amount of
items.explanation: 'A little' and 'a small amount of' mean some, which fits the context.

items.sentence: ___ guards was injured in the attack.
items.explanation: The verb 'was' is singular, so 'Both' (plural) is incorrect. 'Neither' or 'Neither of the' can take a singular verb.

items.sentence: ___ of the clothes were filthy.
items.explanation: 'Clothes' is plural, so 'Much' (singular) is wrong. The others fit.

items.sentence: ___ of the glasses had been smashed.
items.explanation: 'None of the', 'Half of the', and 'Each of the' are correct. 'Every' must be followed by a noun, and 'Whole' usually requires 'the whole of the'.

items.sentence: ___ student in the class whose surname begins with a vowel, please stand up.
items.explanation: 'Each' and 'Every' are followed by singular nouns like 'student'.

items.sentence: There's ___ we can do about the situation, I'm afraid.
items.explanation: All three refer to an uncountable amount of 'what we can do'. 'Not many' is for countable.

items.sentence: We've got ___ time, don't worry.
items.explanation: 'Plenty', 'a lot', and 'lots' all mean 'enough' or 'much' of an uncountable noun.

exercises.label: G
exercises.instruction: Complete the sentences with a, an or the. If no article is required, put a dash (-).

items.sentence: I think there's ___ chemist open in ___ main road somewhere.
items.explanation: A chemist (not specific); the main road (specific location).

items.sentence: One of ___ people who I met at ___ lecture ___ last night is ___ architect for quite ___ well-known firm.
items.explanation: The people (specific); the lecture (specific); last night (no article); an architect (occupation); a firm (one of many).

items.sentence: ___ quality screwdriver is ___ essential part of any tool box.
items.explanation: Generic singular countable nouns.

items.sentence: Who designed ___ invitations for ___ wedding?
items.explanation: Specific invitations for a specific wedding.

items.sentence: ___ poor and ___ elderly are often left behind when it comes to ___ modern technology.
items.explanation: The + adjective (group of people); modern technology (concept, no article).

items.sentence: When I was at ___ school, we had to wear ___ shorts until we were ___ 11 and then we were allowed to wear ___ pair of long trousers.
items.explanation: At school (as a student); wear shorts (general plural); age 11 (no article); a pair (singular).

items.sentence: I met ___ writer ___ Catherine Hawkins ___ other day. She signed ___ copy of her ___ latest book for me.
items.explanation: The writer (specifying who); the other day (idiom); a copy (one of many); latest (superlative usually takes 'the', but 'her latest' uses a possessive instead).

items.sentence: I honestly believe that ___ car is one of ___ worst inventions in ___ history of ___ world.
items.explanation: The car (general type); the worst (superlative); the history (specific); the world (unique).

items.sentence: Is that ___ ewe or ___ elk?
items.explanation: Ewe starts with 'y' sound (consonant); elk starts with vowel sound.

items.sentence: What's ___ difference between ___ unicorn and ___ postbox? You don't know? Then, I'm not going to ask you to post ___ letter for me!
items.explanation: The difference (specific); a unicorn (one of many); a postbox; a letter.

items.sentence: Are these windows really not made of ___?
items.explanation: Material (uncountable) takes no article.

items.sentence: I fancy playing poker. Have we got a ___ of cards anywhere?
items.explanation: A 'pack of cards' is the standard collective noun.

items.sentence: Luckily, only ___ of the medicine got spilt.
items.explanation: Uncountable noun (medicine) requires 'a little' to mean 'some'.

items.sentence: The ___ on the kitchen table.
items.explanation: 'Groceries' is a plural uncountable noun and takes a plural verb.

items.sentence: Hardly ___ of the paintings at the gallery were for sale.
items.explanation: 'Hardly any' means almost none.

items.sentence: It's ___ opportunity to see African wildlife in its natural environment.
items.explanation: 'Unique' starts with a 'y' consonant sound.

items.sentence: You can't just demand ___ you have to earn it.
items.explanation: 'Respect' is an uncountable abstract noun used generally here.

items.sentence: Almost ___ person I've asked says they're going on the anti-war demonstration next Saturday.
items.explanation: 'Every' is used with singular nouns for general groups.

exercises.label: I
exercises.instruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: In publishing, (1) ___ author will usually earn royalties on sales.
items.explanation: Mentioning a person generally for the first time.

items.sentence: For (2) ___ book sold, the author gets a percentage.
items.explanation: Used with singular nouns to mean all in a set.

items.sentence: and (3) ___ bigger the percentage they can get.
items.explanation: Part of the 'the ... the ...' comparative structure.

items.sentence: it is quite possible for a successful novelist to earn a (4) ___ deal of money.
items.explanation: 'A great deal of' is a quantifier for uncountable nouns.

items.sentence: You only have to look at (5) ___ children's author J. K. Rowling to see that.
items.explanation: Used with well-known/famous people.

items.sentence: A painter sells his or her work for (6) ___ fee.
items.explanation: Indefinite article for a singular noun.

items.sentence: (7) ___ time it is sold, its value increases.
items.explanation: Referring to all occasions.

items.sentence: The painter doesn't receive (8) ___ benefit from this, though.
items.explanation: Used in negative sentences.

items.sentence: More than a (9) ___ artists today, however, are very aware of this
items.explanation: 'More than a few' means quite a lot.

items.sentence: but only allow a certain (10) ___ of them, say eight, to be sold.
items.explanation: 'A certain number of' for countable nouns (paintings).

items.sentence: The other two they keep, in (11) ___ hope that when they sell them in later years
items.explanation: Specific hope.

items.sentence: However, since (12) ___ illegal transfer of digital music via the Internet
items.explanation: Specific phenomenon.

items.sentence: (13) ___ songwriters and performers have become worried
items.explanation: Referring to two groups mentioned.

items.sentence: Certainly, the (14) ___ music industry
items.explanation: Meaning all of the industry.

items.sentence: There is still a huge (15) ___ of money to be made from live performances.
items.explanation: 'Amount' for uncountable nouns (money).
